topics.title: Types & Constants vs Variables
topics.description: Understand Swift's basic types and the difference between let (constants) and var (variables).

lessons.title: let vs var
lessons.body: Use let when a value won't change — Swift encourages immutability. Use var only when reassignment is needed.
lessons.code:
let pi = 3.14159
var score = 0
score = 10  // OK
// pi = 3    // Error: cannot assign to a 'let' constant

lessons.title: Basic types
lessons.body: Int, Double, String, Bool are the core value types. Swift infers the type from the initial value.
lessons.code:
let age: Int = 30
let name = "Alice"   // inferred String
let isActive = true  // inferred Bool

lessons.title: String interpolation
lessons.code: let greeting = "Hello, \(name)! You are \(age) years old."

lessons.title: Type safety
lessons.body: Swift will not implicitly convert types. You must convert explicitly.
lessons.code:
let x: Int = 5
let y: Double = Double(x) + 1.5

topics.title: Optionals
topics.description: Master Swift's optional type system to handle the absence of a value safely.

lessons.title: What is an optional?
lessons.body: An optional variable can hold a value or be nil. Declare it with a ? after the type. You cannot use an optional value directly — you must unwrap it.

lessons.title: Optional binding with if let
lessons.body: if let safely unwraps an optional. The code inside runs only if the optional has a value.

lessons.title: guard let
lessons.body: guard let is preferred inside functions. It exits the function early if the optional is nil.

lessons.title: Nil coalescing ??
lessons.body: The ?? operator provides a default value when an optional is nil.

topics.title: Protocols & Protocol Extensions
topics.description: Define interfaces with protocols and add default implementations via protocol extensions.

lessons.title: Defining a protocol
lessons.body: A protocol defines a blueprint of methods and properties. Any type can conform to it — structs, classes, and enums.

lessons.title: Conforming to a protocol
lessons.body: Declare conformance after the type name, then implement all requirements.

lessons.title: Protocol extensions
lessons.body: Add default implementations in an extension. Conforming types get the default behaviour for free.

lessons.title: Protocol composition
lessons.body: Combine multiple protocols with & to express compound requirements.

topics.title: Swift Concurrency: async/await & Actors
topics.description: Write safe, readable concurrent code using Swift's structured concurrency model.

lessons.title: async/await basics
lessons.body: Mark a function async to indicate it can suspend. Use await to call it — the current task suspends, freeing the thread for other work.

lessons.title: Structured concurrency with async let
lessons.body: async let starts a child task immediately. await the results later to collect them concurrently.

lessons.title: Actors
lessons.body: An actor is a reference type that serialises access to its mutable state. Only one task runs inside an actor at a time, preventing data races.

lessons.title: MainActor
lessons.body: @MainActor marks code that must run on the main thread. Use it on UI-related classes and methods to keep updates thread-safe.
